item.title: Multivariable Statistics
item.dc:creator: Ishaan Shrivastava
item.description:
While trying to understand what a privileged-basis means, I realised that I am significantly underequipped with the prerequisites. These are my notes for all the problems I have solved so far, and the things that I have learnt. I intend to turn this into something that is more like an index of theorems once I am more familiar with everything in here.

Prerequisite Notes for the ReLU Privileged-Basis Problem

This document records:

  1. the concepts introduced,
  2. the questions attempted,
  3. my original solutions,
  4. what was correct,
  5. what was incorrect or incomplete,
  6. the corrected derivations.

Question 1 — Covariance and second moments

  • Let be a random vector with mean . We know that, the covariance of (not necessarily independent) two random variables is expressed as: . We can use this notion of covariance of two random variables to construct the notion of covariance of a vector with itself: or, in matrix form, equivalently:

  • The diagonal entries are called the coordinate variances

  • The Euclidean norm is expressed as

  • Trivial Identity:

  • Inner and Outer producs: For vectors

    • The standard Euclidean inner product is equivalently expressed as .
    • The Outer product is

  • for vectors

  • Using the above trivial identity, one can derive another useful identity -

  • Useful -

  • Linearity of Expectation - For integrable random variables and constants

  • If and are independent and the relevant expectations exist, then . Independence is sufficient, but not necessary.

  • The second moment of a random variable is simply the expectation

  • For two random variables it is simple to see that

  • Using the above, it can be seen that independence implies zero covariance - just expand the terms inside and write them elementwise for the entire probability space (use summation or integrals)

item.title: WTF is going on inside Gemma? Thoughts, and some experiments with hybrid post-pre-norm and Per-Layer-Embeddings (PLE)
item.dc:creator: Ishaan Shrivastava
item.description:
Does anyone have any idea of what Gemma-style Per-Layer-Embeddings actually do? Lets pull up Gemma 4 E4B for a closer look.

Google itself doesn’t offer much on this matter1, so let me explain. It’s simple - you can offload the per-layer embeddings to the CPU without impacting throughput. Think of it like a form of sparsity, different in nature from Mixture of Experts (MoE), since we are improving the capabilities of a model by adding additional sets of embeddings at every single layer, instead of scaling the model width or depth. This allows us to store the entire lookup table on flash storage (a whopping 3.5B params out of 8B!) rather than needing valuable VRAM meant for storing the rest of the model parameters.

Let’s do a back-of-the-envelope calculation on the memory transferred between the CPU-offloaded embedding table in the case. The core idea here is to observe that the memory traffic for the embeddings being sent between devices is far less than the whole size of the PLE table.

whereas

which is 6 orders of magnitude smaller than the entire embedding table! Thus, we can index the embedding table on the CPU and then send the bytes over to the device for layerwise inference, and it doesn’t end up becoming a inference bottleneck.

I couldn’t find code to do this on the HuggingFace version of Gemma 3n, so I wrote my own little snippet to do this using pytorch hooks - Full reproducible script annotated with comments here

Gemma3nForConditionalGeneration.from_pretrained('google/gemma-3n-e2b-it', torch_dtype=torch.bfloat16,).to('cuda').eval()

lm = model.model.language_model
lm.embed_tokens_per_layer.to(device="cpu", dtype=torch.float16)

@torch.no_grad()
def get_per_layer_inputs_cpu(self, input_ids: torch.LongTensor) -> torch.Tensor:
    ids_cpu = input_ids.to("cpu", non_blocking=True)
    ple_cpu = self.embed_tokens_per_layer(ids_cpu)
    ple_cpu = ple_cpu.reshape(*ids_cpu.shape, self.config.num_hidden_layers, self.hidden_size_per_layer_input,)
    return ple_cpu.to(device=input_ids.device, dtype=torch.bfloat16, non_blocking=True)

lm.get_per_layer_inputs = types.MethodType(get_per_layer_inputs_cpu, lm)
del model.model.audio_tower, model.model.embed_audio
gc.collect()
torch.cuda.empty_cache()

generation = model.generate(**inputs, max_new_tokens=100, do_sample=False)
print(processor.decode(generation[0]), skip_special_tokens=True)

# **Overall Impression:** The image is a close-up shot of a vibrant garden scene,
# focusing on a cluster of pink cosmos flowers and a busy bumblebee.
# It has a slightly soft, natural feel, likely captured in daylight.

Here are the VRAM savings, the GPU usage visibly drops right after the hook is applied, seen through nvtop

Before CPU offload:

After CPU offload:

PLE performance gains?

It is not immediately obvious HOW PLE improves performance. Why even bother? What problem do they solve at this model scale? All I’ve been able to find so far on this matter are sloppy medium posts and I don’t trust them. I spent a good while trying to figure it out… honestly, I still don’t know for sure, I think it would make for a great mech-interp paper. Let’s try to work this out anyway!

My rough understanding of transformers is that attention approximates computation and MLP approximates memory. Looking at the architecture diagram, we can see two things:

  1. Residual stream gating the PLE lookup - A simple hypothesis I agree with2 is that the layer-wise lookup allows the model to store and recall a great deal of information as the lookup is precomputed for every forward pass, instead of going through matmuls in the MLP, thereby increasing capacity without commensurate increases in VRAM occupancy.

  2. Mix-LayerNorm (pre + post) - The transformer blocks use post-norm as well as pre-norm. The disadvantage of Post-norm is that it keeps the L2 norm of the hidden state activations approximately constant across layers as there is no identity path, which means signals originating from early layers keep shrinking as they propagate forward, leading to vanishing influence on the output (and, correspondingly, vanishing gradients). This is a well known problem with post-norm, which is why most LLMs these days use pre-norm as it avoids training instability issues with increase in depth reference - blog by Ziming Liu. Since gemma uses both, it might still suffer from the described post-norm limitation. I have a suspicion that if post-norm were not used in the Gemma architecture, these per-layer embeddings would correspondingly not also have been necessary (simply allow information from first layer token embeddings to flow through the residual stream and reach every layer normally).

Implementing PLE - from scratch (it works!)

Out of curiosity, I cooked up a small-scale transformer baseline on the shakespeare corpus, modeled as NTP on raw byte pairs (yes, I didn’t use a tokenizer, literally next-byte prediction). Here are some results:

  - prenorm=False, postnorm=False
      - ple_dim=0: 1.601218
      - ple_dim=8: 1.571632
      - ple_dim=32: 1.550698
      - ple_dim=128: 1.622619

  - prenorm=False, postnorm=True
      - ple_dim=0: 1.581143
      - ple_dim=8: 1.595611
      - ple_dim=32: 1.579468
      - ple_dim=128: 1.559271

  - prenorm=True, postnorm=False
      - ple_dim=0: 1.541992
      - ple_dim=8: 1.546094
      - ple_dim=32: 1.537176
      - ple_dim=128: 1.529346

  - prenorm=True, postnorm=True
      - ple_dim=0: 1.541877
      - ple_dim=8: 1.544192
      - ple_dim=32: 1.534553
      - ple_dim=128: 1.528164

  Averaged across all four norm settings
  - ple_dim=0: 1.566558
  - ple_dim=8: 1.564382
  - ple_dim=32: 1.550474
  - ple_dim=128: 1.559850

Ignoring the weird error bars on the rightmost chart for x=8, we can see that the average performance over validation generally improves with increase in PLE dimension which tells us that PLE does indeed improve performance on this simple toy task.

wtf is MatFormer?

I do not have the motivation nor understanding to explain what it is, but it has something to do with training nested subsets of the FFN blocks in such a way that they can be sliced width-wise in each layer with only negligible degradation. I’ve made some (minor) fixes to the demo colab - https://colab.research.google.com/drive/1g3S_On6aDHmHmeiRwf_-bI0yZvPiJelF

Do try it out. I’m tryna find good subsets smaller than E2B rn, here’s two I found that inferenced just fiiine. - capriciousmarauder/test-submodel-3 This one is more sliced than E2B. - capriciousmarauder/test-submodel-4 This one is very aggressively sliced, even more than the other one.

# score for google/gemma-3n-E2B-it, google's official published slice of Gemma3n-E4B-it
{
    "en_text_recognition": 0.6096878412118829,
    "en_text_detection": 0.23172620479653033,
    "en_text_spotting": 0.0,
    "en_element_parsing": 0.04,
    "en_mathematical_calculation": 0.3240026170865559,
    "en_visual_text_understanding": 0.7103983997859018,
    "en_knowledge_reasoning": 0.5074402603391522,
    "English Overall Score": 0.34617933188857475
}

# score for capriciousmarauder/test-submodel-3, which is a slice of Gemma3n-E4B-it
{
    "en_text_recognition": 0.5459304480487074,
    "en_text_detection": 0.21590682007667475,
    "en_text_spotting": 0.0,
    "en_element_parsing": 0.0275,
    "en_mathematical_calculation": 0.2693079603436732,
    "en_visual_text_understanding": 0.676684722674171,
    "en_knowledge_reasoning": 0.4669721878768469,
    "English Overall Score": 0.3146145912885819
}

# score for capriciousmarauder/test-submodel-4, which is a slice of Gemma3n-E4B-it
{
    "en_text_recognition": 0.34489594363685844,
    "en_text_detection": 0.14555663691294746,
    "en_text_spotting": 0.0,
    "en_element_parsing": 0.02,
    "en_mathematical_calculation": 0.20092149837002324,
    "en_visual_text_understanding": 0.5540588220515938,
    "en_knowledge_reasoning": 0.44284559706420745,
    "English Overall Score": 0.2440397854336615
}

Footnotes

  1. Google generously spent the equivalent of a paragraph worth of tokens to explain Per-Layer-Embeddings in their blog on Gemma 3n last year. Recently, they one-upped themselves, offering a single sentence worth of information about PLE in the docs for their latest open-source model series, Gemma 4 - link here. In other words, they offer zero explanation about why it improves the performance over simply injecting the embeddings into the residual stream, or why they could not have simply kept a normal embedding table and just made it larger, and so on.↩︎

  2. The credit for this particular hypothesis as well as elicitation of a lot of the discussion in the blog is thanks to aletheia who is the creator of Mirage, a custom zeroth-order optimization library for Episteme, a superhuman UCI chess engine - check out their blog!↩︎

item.title: Part-Whole training
item.dc:creator: Ishaan Shrivastava
item.description:
Reference - https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/abs/pii/0001691889900073?via%3Dihub

This is a common approach to the training of a task that is difficult and demanding and cannot be initially coped with.

Under this approach, the whole task is decomposed into segments.

There are several strategies to decompose tasks -

  • segmentation - divide a task meaningfully across time.
  • fractionation - breaking down task into elements
  • simplification - reduction in difficulty

Pitfalls

Training individual schemas has a negative transfer to performance on the whole task, as it is theorized that the main properties of schemas lie in the connections between them (which are not developed subsequently).

item.title: Reproducing Attention is All you Need, from scratch - PyTorch
item.dc:creator: Ishaan Shrivastava
item.description:
How to understand transformer in one image?

  • Here is a useful diagram explaining the transformer encoder in great detail.

What is a Transformer?

Transformers are a sequence modelling and transduction architecture heavily leveraging self-attention in order to eliminate the need for convolutions or gating. Advantages over RNNs and LSTMs include:

  1. Non recurrent computation - Computation for a layer at a given depth is completely parallelized, while in prior architectures the computation at a given position and depth required computing for all the depths before them.
  2. Global dependency modelling - Prior architectures featured a growth in computation required to relate information between positions with larger distance between them. Transformers do away with this limitation by modelling dependencies in a global fashion.
  3. Permutation invariance - The transformer architecture fundamentally looks at its inputs as a set of elements1, as opposed to a sequence. This makes it incredibly powerful and widely applicable.

Transformer Architecture

The transformer architecture features an encoder-decoder setup in the fashion of a typical neural machine translation task. The encoder maps a sequence of symbol representations to a sequence of continuous representations . Given , the decoder then generates the output sequence of symbols in an auto-regressive fashion.

Let us introduce the building blocks of the transformer architecture. ### 1. Scaled dot-product attention The output is calculated as a weighted sum of values of dimension . The weights are softmaxed scores, and the scores are obtained by computing the dot-product of an input query with the key vector associated with that value, each being of dimension .

… which yields the scaled dot-product attention formula:

… which, in practice, is further vectorized:

$$O = \text{Attention}(Q, K, V) = \text{softmax}\bigg(\dfrac{Q\cdot K}{\sqrt{d_k}}\bigg)\cdot V$$

2. Multi-head attention

It is found that a single attention function with dimensional keys, queries and values can be improved upon. The input matrices and are projected to , and is projected to , and this is done multiple times across different heads2. The scaled dot-product attention outputs of each head are concatenated and finally projected once again.

3. Types of Attention

  • The encoder uses multi-head self-attention layers. In self-attention, all of the keys, queries, and values come from the same place, i.e. the output of the previous encoder layer.
  • The decoder uses two kinds of multi-head attention layers - cross-attention takes in keys and values from the encoder outputs, while the queries come from MHSA earlier within the same decoder block. This particular choice allows the decoder to relate the current position with representations from all positions of the encoder.
  • Additionally, the decoder uses self-attention layers with masking in order to allow each position in the decoder to attend to all positions up to and including that position. This is done to prevent leftward information flow in the decoder to preserve the auto-regressive property.3

4. Position-wise feed forward networks

Each encoder/decoder block applies a fully-connected feed-forward network on each position, identically and separately.

The dimensionality of input and output is , while that of the middle layer is

5. Positional encoding

Multi-head attention cannot keep track of temporal information unless explicitly given.4 In order for the model to make use of the order of the sequence, we must inject some information about the relative or absolute position of elements in the sequence. To achieve this, we add positional embeddings to the input embeddings. These embeddings carry the same dimension as the embeddings, allowing them to be summed.

6. Tokenizer

The tokenizer converts

Footnotes

Footnotes

  1. One crucial characteristic of the multi-head self-attention is that it is permutation-invariant with respect to its inputs. This means that if we switch between two inputs elements in the sequence, e.g. X1 <-> X2, the output is exactly the same besides the elements 1 and 2 switched. Read more here.↩︎

  2. Each head can be thought of as computing information from different representation subspaces, attending information from different positions. Using some inspiration from Toy Models of Superposition, I opine that a head could be thought of as a coarse computation across different feature sets, which would benefit the availability of different heads.↩︎

  3. I suppose that information flows where the gradient goes? I do not completely understand the reasons motivating this choice.↩︎

  4. One crucial characteristic of the multi-head self-attention is that it is permutation-invariant with respect to its inputs. This means that if we switch between two inputs elements in the sequence, e.g. X1 <-> X2, the output is exactly the same besides the elements 1 and 2 switched. Read more here.↩︎

item.title: Rotating Emphasis Manipulation
item.dc:creator: Ishaan Shrivastava
item.description:
Reference - https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/abs/pii/0001691889900073?via%3Dihub

Why

The core of expert performance is an organised set of response strategies than can be employed flexibly to meet task demands.

Subjects trained under rotating emphasis manipulation performed significantly better than control players, and experienced a continued advantage increase after experiment termination.

What

The schema model of executive function observes the formation of schemas, individual and indivisible units of control that can be composed together (many, and often multiple at the same time together) in order to meet the demands of a complex task.

When performing a complicated task, human subjects often have little knowledge of, and do not spontaneously explore, alternative strategies. When led to do so, performance improves.

Performance under high-attention load conditions benefits most when subjects are trained on several, varying levels of subelement priorities.

How

Divide skills into core components, train individually, together and then experiments with their composition. This is aptly called Part-Whole training.

However, instead of training individual schemas in isolation of each other and in absence of the entire goal, only attempt to manipulate and assign the relative emphasis (priority) of selected subcomponents in a rotating manner.

item.title: speculative-decoding
item.dc:creator: Ishaan Shrivastava
item.description:
Algorithm for greedy sampling

TODO: make the sampling safe for zero division errors

TODO: add stopping condition (max_new_tokens, EOS token) for verifier and draft model as pointed out in both the model forward pass line comments

TODO: implement get_probs_from_logits

TODO: convert logits to probs, add temperature

TODO: add analysis of temperature to acceptance rate

TODO: add SFT simple for both

TODO: add RL with acceptance rate

def remove_last_t_from_kvcache(kvcache, t): for i, (k_layer, v_layer) in enumerate(kvcache): if k_layer is not None: kvcache[i] = ( k_layer[:, :, :-t, :], # remove last num_to_remove keys v_layer[:, :, :-t, :] # remove last num_to_remove values ) ““” 1. Inference loop: - inputs object with keys “input_ids” and “attention_mask” # TODO: code currently works for mask of shape [1, L] but won’t for a 3d [1, L, L] square mask. - past_key_values_draft : (DynamicCache(config=draft_model.config)) - past_key_values_verifier: (DynamicCache(config=draft_model.config)) - k = 10 - max_new_tokens = 100 ““”

initialize generated_ids for the recursion

generated_ids = inputs[“input_ids”]

infer draft model k times

start_position = 0 if past_key_values_draft[0][0] is None else past_key_values_draft[0].shape[-2] draft_cache_position = torch.arange(start_position, inputs.input_ids.shape[1], dtype=torch.int64, device=draft_model.device) draft_logits = torch.empty_like(inputs.input_ids[:, 0:0]) # tensor of shape [N, 0]

for draft_iter in range(k): outputs = draft_model(**inputs, cache_position=draft_cache_position, past_key_values=past_key_values_draft, use_cache=True) # TODO: add stopping condition here.

# sampling - greedy
next_token_ids = outputs.logits[:, -1].argmax(-1)
draft_logits = torch.concat([draft_logits, outputs.logits[:, -1:, :]], dim=-2)

generated_ids = torch.cat([generated_ids, next_token_ids], dim=-1)
attention_mask = inputs["attention_mask"]
attention_mask = torch.cat([attention_mask, attention_mask.new_ones((attention_mask.shape[0], 1))], dim=-1)
inputs = {"input_ids": next_token_ids, "attention_mask": attention_mask}
draft_cache_position = draft_cache_position[-1:] + 1 # add one more position for the next token to be decoded.

run verifier in parallel

inputs = {“input_ids”: generated_ids, “attention_mask”: inputs[“attention_mask”]} start_position = 0 if past_key_values_verifier[0][0] is None else past_key_values_verifier[0].shape[-2] verifier_cache_position = torch.arange(start_position, inputs.input_ids.shape[1], dtype=torch.int64, device=verifier_model.device) outputs = verifier_model(**inputs, cache_position=verifier_cache_position, past_key_values=past_key_values_verifier, use_cache=True) # TODO: add stopping condition here.

determine the number of accepted guesses n

verifier_logits = outputs.logits[:, -(draft_logits.shape[-2]+1):-1, :] verifier_probs = verifier_logits.softmax(-1) draft_probs = draft_logits.softmax(-1) prob_ratios = verifier_probs / draft_probs # TODO: make the sampling safe for zero division errors? prob_ratios_per_token = torch.gather(prob_ratios, dim=-2, index=generated_ids.unsqueeze(dim=-2))

sample tokens until rejection criteria is met

r1 ∼ U(0, 1), . . . , rγ ∼ U(0, 1)

uniform_mask = torch.randn_like(prob_ratios_per_token) > prob_ratios_per_token uniform_mask = torch.concat([uniform_mask, torch.ones(uniform_mask.shape[0], 1, dtype=torch.bool)]) # verifier token index, if all accept. ### n ← min({i − 1 | 1 ≤ i ≤ γ, ri > pi(x) / qi(x)} ∪ {γ}) n = argmax(uniform_mask, dim=-1)

destroy the last (k-n) key-values from draft kvcache

note that this implementation is not batched.

remove_last_t_from_kvcache(past_key_values_draft, k-n)

destroy the last (k-n) key-values from verifier kvcache

remove_last_t_from_kvcache(past_key_values_verifier, k-n)

adjust the final token from verifier if needed

if n < k: extra_token_logits = norm * (max(0, draft_logits[:, -1-(k-n), :] - verifier_logits[:, -1-(k-n), :])) else: extra_token_logits = verifier_logits[:, -1:, :] extra_token_logits = extra_token_logits.unsqueeze(1)

sample one extra token from adjusted distribution

sampling - greedy

extra_token = extra_token_logits.argmax(-1)

add the generated ids to inputs object to close the recursion, and adjust attention mask. rebuild attention mask entirely?

generated_ids = torch.concat([generated_ids, extra_token], dim=-1) attention_mask = attention_mask.new_ones((attention_mask.shape[0], generated_ids.shape[-1])) inputs = {“inputs”: generated_ids, “attention_mask”: attention_mask}

if q(x) <= p(x): q(x) if q(x) > p(x): - sample (p(x) / q(x)) * ( q(x) ) + (1 - p(x) / q(x)) * ( norm(max(0, p(x) - q(x))) )

desired probability of selecting x: p(x) actual selection: q(x)

distribution that is being sampled from is:

note: alpha is the normalization constant multiplied with the random variable: alpha * (max(0, p(x) - q(x))) to get a probability distribution.

  1. q(x) + alpha

let us suppose there are two groups of events for the decoding of an N-vocabulary.

A = {xk : p(xi) >= q(xi) for i in range(N)} R = {xk : p(xi) < q(xi) for i in range(N)}

now, say that the probability that x ~ q(x) is in A is pA = sum( q(xi) * (1 if p(xi) >= q(xi) else 0) ) and, say that the probability that x ~ p(x) is in R is pR = sum( q(xi) * (1 if p(xi) < q(xi) else 0) )

now, we need to show that the final sampling results in the final token sampling probability being equal to p(x).

probability of a token xt (from set A) being sampled = pA * ( q(xt) / pA ) + pR * rejection probability * probabilty that xt is resampled from adjusted dist. = pA * (q(xt) / pA) + pR * rejection probability * norm * max(0, p(xt) - q(xt)) = q(xt) + pR * rejection probability * norm * (p(xt) - q(xt))

now, we need to compute norm and rejection prob. note: rejection probability in the above is conditioned on sample being from B. so lets compute it.

note: P is not p. P is probability of any event function while p is the verifier distribution on tokens. rejection prob. = P(sampled token is rejected | sampled token is from R) = [P(rejected and from R)] / [P(from R)] = [ sum( q(xi) * (1 - p(xi) / q(xi)) if p(xi) < q(xi) else 0) ] / pR = [ sum(q(xi) - p(xi) if p(xi) < q(xi) else 0) ] / pR

-> probability of a token xt from set A being sampled = q(xt) + [ sum(q(xi) - p(xi) if p(xi) < q(xi) else 0) ] * norm * (p(xt) - q(xt))

so, the normalizing constant norm comes out to be: norm = 1 / [ sum(q(xi) - p(xi) if p(xi) < q(xi) else 0) ]

and, we have:

-> probability of a token xt from set A being sampled = q(xt) + (p(xt) - q(xt)) = p(xt)

similarly, let us compute: probability of a token xt (from set R) being sampled = pR * ( q(xt) / pR) * acceptance probability = q(xt) * ( p(xt) / q(xt) ) = p(xt)

hence, we have proven the correctness of the sampling scheme and also found the normalizing constant.

item.title: Measure Theory
item.dc:creator: Ishaan Shrivastava
item.description:
-Algebra

A sigma algebra on a set is a nonempty set of subsets of that is closed under complement, countable union, and countable intersection. In other words:

$$\begin{aligned} &\mathcal{A} \subseteq \mathcal{P}(X) \text{ is a } \sigma\text{-algebra on }X\text{ if:} \\ (a) \text{ } &\phi, X \in \mathcal{A}& \\ (b) \text{ } & A \in \mathcal{A} \implies A^c \coloneqq X \setminus A \in \mathcal{A} \\ (c) \text{ } & A_i \in \mathcal{A}, i \in \mathbb{N} \implies \bigcup^\infty_{i=1} A_i \in \mathcal{A}\end{aligned}$$

is also called a -measurable set. In other words, a measure (which is a kind of generalised notion of volume) can be assigned to a -algebra. This notion is formally established later in Measure

Borel -Algebra

For every , there is a smallest -algebra that contains :

Note that uncountable unions of -algebras () are again valid -algebras. Easily provable using the original -algebra definition.

A Borel -algebra on a topological space (noted as ) is the -algebra generated by the open sets . In other words:

These Borel -algebras have some interesting properties that allow us to define a measure, or a generalised notion of volume, upon them, which we will later find is impossible to do on the entire powerset

Measure

Let us say that is a measurable space.

$$\begin{aligned} &\text{A map } \mu \colon \mathcal{A} \rightarrow [0, \infty) \cup\{\infty\} \text{ is called a measure if:} \\ (a) \space & \mu(\phi) =  0\\ (b) \space & \mu\biggl(\bigcup_{i=1}^n A_i \biggr) =  \sum_{i=1}^{n}\sigma(A_i), \space\space\space\space\space\space\space\space \text{with }A_i \cap A_j = \phi, i \neq j \text{ }\forall \text{ countable sequences } A_i \in \mathcal{A}\end{aligned}$$

Collectively, are referred to as a measure space.

item.title: ANSWER KEY TO PRIVILEGED BASIS AND RELU QUIZ
item.description:
1 answer sheet

Here is the answer sheet - index.qmd

2 Grading rule

Each main question is worth (2) points.

  • (0): absent, materially false, circular, or confuses object types;
  • (1): correct central idea with a missing definition, assumption, or proof step;
  • (2): correct, explicit, and type-consistent.

Equivalent formulations are acceptable.

3 Part I — Measurability and information

3.1 Answer 1

A sigma-algebra (F^) satisfies:

  1. (F);
  2. (EFE^cF);
  3. (E_nF) for all (n_{n=1}^E_nF).

A probability space is a triple

where (F) is a sigma-algebra and

satisfies

and countable additivity on pairwise disjoint events.

3.2 Answer 2

For (G):

  • (,G);
  • complements swap ({1,2}) and ({3,4});
  • every countable union reduces to one of the four listed sets.

Thus (G) is a sigma-algebra.

For (H), note

Therefore (H) is not closed under unions and is not a sigma-algebra.

3.3 Answer 3

(X) is measurable iff

Preimages are used because they preserve complements and arbitrary unions:

Images do not generally preserve complements or intersections in the required way.

3.4 Answer 4

For any (CS),

Since (f) is measurable,

Since (X) is measurable,

Hence (fX) is measurable.

3.5 Answer 5

The fibres of (X) are

Therefore ((X)) consists of every union of these fibres:

Observing (X) distinguishes the three fibres but cannot distinguish (_1) from (_2).

3.6 Answer 6

The atomic masses are

Therefore:

3.7 Answer 7

In the real- or Euclidean-valued setting used here:

An estimator that uses only the information contained in (A) must be ((A))-measurable. The theorem then gives

for some measurable (g). Conversely, every measurable (g(A)) is ((A))-measurable.

3.8 Answer 8

  1. (A) is a measurable map (R^d).
  2. (a) is a deterministic vector in (R^d).
  3. ((A)) is a collection of subsets of (), specifically a sub-sigma-algebra of (F).
  4. ({AB}=A^{-1}(B)) is an event in (F).
  5. (E[ZA]) is a random vector, defined up to almost-sure equality, and is ((A))-measurable.

3.9 Answer 9

The map

is linear and continuous, hence Borel measurable.

Coordinatewise ReLU

is continuous, hence Borel measurable.

Since (Z) is measurable and compositions of measurable maps are measurable,

is measurable.

4 Part II — Conditional expectation

4.1 Answer 10

A conditional expectation (Y=E[XG]) satisfies:

  1. (Y) is (G)-measurable;
  2. (YL^1);
  3. for every (GG),

It is unique up to almost-sure equality.

4.2 Answer 11

The two atoms of (G) are

On (G_1),

On (G_2),

Hence

4.3 Answer 12

The events in (G) are

For (), both sides are zero.

For (G_1),

while

For (G_2),

while

For (), add the two block identities.

4.4 Answer 13

(X) itself is (G)-measurable and integrable. For every (GG),

Thus (X) satisfies the defining properties of (E[XG]). By almost-sure uniqueness,

4.5 Answer 14

The constant (E[X]) is (G)-measurable. For (GG), independence gives

Also,

Hence the constant (E[X]) satisfies the defining property.

4.6 Answer 15

Let

Then (Y) is (H)-measurable.

For every (HH), since (HG),

Thus (Y) satisfies the defining property of (E[XH]). Uniqueness gives

4.7 Answer 16

Let

Write

Then

The cross term is zero because (m-E[X]) is (G)-measurable:

Also,

and

4.8 Answer 17

Let

Use

and expand the outer product. The cross terms vanish because

Thus

Expectation of a random matrix is taken entrywise.

4.9 Answer 18

By definition,

Using linearity of trace and conditional expectation,

Taking expectations and applying the tower property gives

4.10 Answer 19

If (A>0), then (Z=A).

If (A=0), then (Z), and conditionally (Z) is uniform on ([-1,0]). Therefore

and

Hence

The conditional variance is

Since (P(A=0)=1/2),

4.11 Answer 20

If (A>0), then (Z=A).

If (A=0), then (Z), so

Thus

All conditional error occurs on (A=0). Therefore

4.12 Answer 21

For continuously distributed (A),

for each fixed (a), so the elementary ratio divides by zero.

The primary object is instead

defined by measurability and integral identities. Under standard regularity conditions it has a version

and one may use (h(a)) as a version of (E[ZA=a]). Values of (h) on sets of (A)-measure zero are arbitrary.

4.13 Answer 22

Since (Z) almost surely,

Therefore

and the MMSE is zero.

5 Part III — (L^2) geometry and MMSE

5.1 Answer 23

where

The inner product and norm are

If (X=Y) a.s., then

A normed space must identify zero-distance objects.

5.2 Answer 24

is the set of square-integrable (G)-measurable random variables modulo a.s. equality.

If (X,Y) are (G)-measurable, then

is (G)-measurable for (a,bR), and remains square-integrable. Hence it is a linear subspace.

The projection theorem requires the subspace to be closed. This subspace is indeed closed in (L^2).

5.3 Answer 25

Let (M) be a nonempty closed linear subspace of a Hilbert space (H). For each (xH), there is a unique (mM) such that

This (m=P_Mx) is equivalently characterized by

meaning

5.4 Answer 26

Let

For (V=1_G), (GG), the defining property gives

By linearity, this holds for every (G)-measurable simple function (V).

For general (VL^2(G)), choose simple (V_nV) in (L^2). Since (X-mL^2), Cauchy–Schwarz gives

Since each

the limit is zero.

5.5 Answer 27

Write

Therefore

Taking expectations, the cross term is

The vector (m-T) is (G)-measurable, while

Hence

The decomposition follows.

5.6 Answer 28

Let

Every admissible estimator using only (A) is a square-integrable (G)-measurable random vector and therefore has the form (g(A)), up to a.s. equality.

Set

Question 27 gives

The second term is nonnegative and is zero for (g(A)=m). Therefore

The minimizer is unique as an element of (L^2), hence unique up to almost-sure equality.

If a candidate (g(A)) is not square-integrable, its squared risk is infinite or not an admissible finite-risk competitor.

5.7 Answer 29

Let

Then

The second equality is the tower property. The third uses

5.8 Answer 30

Since

we have (L(Q)=0) iff

The right-hand side is ((A))-measurable, so this is equivalent to (Z) being ((A))-measurable up to a.s. equality.

By the factorization theorem, this is equivalent to the existence of measurable (g) with

This only requires recovery on a set carrying probability one. The deterministic observation map may fail to be injective elsewhere, including on points or sets that the law of (Z) never visits.

5.9 Answer 31

The unrestricted class contains every measurable square-integrable function of (A). The affine class contains only

The unrestricted optimum is

Example: let (AN(0,1)) and define

Then (Z) is already known from (A), so

and the unrestricted MMSE is zero.

No affine function (bA+c) equals (A^2) almost surely under a nondegenerate Gaussian law. Hence the best affine risk is strictly positive.

5.10 Answer 32

Let

Since (ZN(0,I_d)) and (Q) is orthogonal,

Also,

and

For any decoder (g), define

This is a bijection between measurable decoders (g) and measurable decoders (h). Since (Q) preserves norms,

Therefore

The right-hand side depends only on the distribution of (Y), which is (N(0,I_d)) for every (Q). Hence (L(Q)) is constant.

6 Bridge problems

6.1 Bridge 1

If (Z) coordinatewise almost surely, then

almost surely. At (Q=I),

so (g(a)=a) reconstructs (Z) exactly. Thus (L(I)=0).

Sparsity and the exponential amplitude distribution are irrelevant because only nonnegativity is used.

6.2 Bridge 2

The collision argument is incomplete because:

  1. the two points may both have probability zero;
  2. one or both points may lie outside the support of (Z);
  3. the collision may occur only on a null subset of latent space;
  4. the conditional law given the shared observation may still be degenerate almost surely;
  5. for continuous distributions, pointwise collisions do not by themselves establish positive conditional variance on a positive-probability set of observations.

The required claim is probabilistic, not merely set-theoretic.

6.3 Bridge 3

Since

and the integrand is nonnegative,

iff

with positive probability.

A concrete sufficient condition is: there exists a measurable set (B) in observation space with

and a constant (>0) such that

almost surely on ({AB}). Then

The uniform lower bound can be weakened; it is enough that the nonnegative conditional trace be positive on a set of positive probability.

6.4 Bridge 4

Here

Since (Z_1,Z_2),

Also,

On the region

we observe

Thus the observation reveals the sum but not the split.

A rigorous positivity proof can proceed by:

  1. restricting to the event that both coordinates are active;
  2. identifying the joint density on (0<z_1<z_2);
  3. changing variables to
  4. showing that, conditional on a nontrivial interval of sums (s), the variable (u) has a nondegenerate conditional density on
  5. concluding that on a positive-probability set of observations;
  6. hence there and (L(Q)>0).

The main technical issue is that conditioning on an exact continuous value of the sum requires a regular conditional distribution or a density calculation. Merely drawing the fibre is not enough.

item.title: Prerequisite Study Guide: ReLU Privileged-Basis Reconstruction
item.dc:creator: Ishaan Shrivastava
item.description:
1 Purpose

The target object is

Your existing notes already cover the algebraic prerequisites:

  • expectation,
  • covariance,
  • independence,
  • finite second moments,
  • norms, traces, and outer products.

This guide covers only the remaining load-bearing material:

  1. measurable spaces, measurable maps, generated sigma-algebras, and laws;
  2. conditional expectation, tower property, and conditional variance;
  3. conditional expectation as an orthogonal projection in (L^2), hence the MMSE theorem.

2 Primary reading

Use:

Nima Moshayedi, Lectures on Probability Theory, University of Zurich

https://arxiv.org/pdf/2010.16280

The page numbers below refer to the printed page numbers in the notes.

2.1 Level 1 — Information and measurability

Read only:

  • Appendix A, §1, Measurable Spaces, pp. 143–144.
  • Appendix A, §3, Borel Sets, pp. 146–147.
  • Appendix A, §5, Measurable Maps, pp. 151–152.
  • Chapter 2, §1 and §1.1, General Definitions and Law of a Random Variable, pp. 25–28.
  • Chapter 2, §2.4 and Proposition 2.2, Sigma-algebras generated by a random variable, pp. 33–34.

You should leave this level knowing precisely:

is measurable iff

and

The crucial factorization theorem is:

for some measurable (f), in the setting used here.

2.2 Level 2 — Conditional expectation

Read only:

  • Chapter 3, §2.2, Discrete construction, pp. 80–82.
  • Chapter 3, §2.3 through Theorem 2.18, General construction and basic properties, pp. 82–84.
  • Theorem 2.29, Tower property, p. 86.

Skip on the first pass:

  • the Radon–Nikodym construction;
  • transition kernels;
  • martingales.

You should leave this level knowing that

is a (G)-measurable random variable satisfying

and that, for (HG),

2.3 Level 3 — (L^2) geometry and MMSE

Read only:

  • Chapter 3, §1, (L^2(,F,P)) as a Hilbert space, pp. 73–75.
  • Chapter 3, §1.2, Orthogonal projection, pp. 78–79.
  • Definitions 2.12 and 2.15 and Remarks 2.13–2.14, pp. 82–83.
  • The first two paragraphs of §4.1.2, pp. 90–91, emphasizing the distinction between:
    • projection onto all ((A))-measurable functions;
    • projection onto the much smaller linear span of the observed variables.

You should leave this level able to prove

This identity is the actual reason conditional expectation is the MMSE decoder.

3 Optional terse reference

For a compact formal reference:

Matija Vidmar, Probability with Measure

https://arxiv.org/pdf/2604.01685

Relevant sections:

  • §1.1, measurable sets;
  • §1.3, measurable maps and generated sigma-fields;
  • §3.3, conditioning.

Do not use this as the first exposition unless you prefer definition-dense notes.

4 How to use the quiz

Do three passes.

  1. Closed-notes pass: attempt every question without reading the answer key.
  2. Open-notes repair pass: repair incomplete definitions and proofs.
  3. Compression pass: rewrite each starred proof in at most ten lines without omitting assumptions.

Each ordinary question is worth (2) points:

  • (0): absent, materially false, or circular;
  • (1): right idea but missing a required definition, assumption, or proof step;
  • (2): correct and explicit.

A starred question is load-bearing. A zero on any starred question means the corresponding level is not yet secure, regardless of total score.

5 Quiz

6 Part I — Measurability and information

6.1 Question 1 ★ — Sigma-algebra

State the definition of a sigma-algebra (F) on a set ().

Then state the definition of a probability space

Do not explain by analogy. Give the axioms.

6.2 Question 2 — Finite examples

Let

and

  1. Prove that (G) is a sigma-algebra.
  2. Let Is (H) a sigma-algebra? Prove your answer.

6.3 Question 3 ★ — Measurable map

Let ((,F)) and ((E,E)) be measurable spaces.

State precisely what it means for

to be measurable.

Why is the definition written using preimages, not images?

6.4 Question 4 — Composition

Let

and

be measurable.

Prove directly from the definition that (fX) is measurable.

6.5 Question 5 ★ — Generated sigma-algebra

Let

and define

Assume the codomain has the full power-set sigma-algebra.

List every element of ((X)).

Interpret ((X)) as the information revealed by observing (X).

6.6 Question 6 — Law or pushforward distribution

Suppose

Using the (X) from Question 5, compute the law

Give its value on every subset of ({0,1,2}).

6.7 Question 7 ★ — Factorization through the observation

State the factorization theorem relating these two statements:

  1. (Y) is ((X))-measurable.
  2. (Y=f(X)) for some measurable function (f).

Use it to explain rigorously why an estimator that is allowed to use only (A) has the form

6.8 Question 8 — Type checking

For a random vector (A:R^d), distinguish the types of:

  1. (A);
  2. (aR^d);
  3. ((A));
  4. ({AB}), where (BB(R^d));
  5. (E[ZA]).

6.9 Question 9 ★ — Measurability of the ReLU observation

Assume

is measurable and (QR^{dd}) is fixed.

Prove that

is measurable.

State every closure fact you use.

7 Part II — Conditional expectation

7.1 Question 10 ★ — Definition

Let (XL^1) and let (GF) be a sub-sigma-algebra.

State the defining properties of

Include the sense in which (Y) is unique.

7.2 Question 11 — Finite partition calculation

Let

with probabilities

and let

on these four outcomes.

Let

Compute (E[XG]) as a random variable on ().

7.3 Question 12 ★ — Verify from the definition

For your answer to Question 11, verify explicitly that

for every (GG).

7.4 Question 13 — Already known information

Prove:

If (X) is (G)-measurable and integrable, then

7.5 Question 14 — Irrelevant information

Prove:

If (X) is integrable and independent of (G), then

7.6 Question 15 ★ — Tower property

Let

and (XL^1).

State and prove

Use the defining property of conditional expectation, not an informal averaging argument.

7.7 Question 16 ★ — Law of total variance

For (XL^2), prove

Define

Identify exactly where the cross term vanishes.

7.8 Question 17 — Vector version

For (ZL^2(;R^d)), prove

All quantities are matrices. State what expectation of a random matrix means.

7.9 Question 18 ★ — Trace of conditional covariance

Let

Prove

Then prove

Do not remove the conditional expectation in the first identity.

7.10 Question 19 — Scalar ReLU with a uniform latent

Let

Compute explicitly:

  1. (E[ZA]) as a function (h(A));
  2. ((ZA));
  3. the MMSE

7.11 Question 20 — Scalar Gaussian ReLU

Let

You may use

Compute (E[ZA]) and the MMSE.

7.12 Question 21 — Conditioning on a continuously distributed observation

Explain why the notation

cannot generally be defined by the elementary formula

when (A) is continuous.

Explain why

remains well-defined.

7.13 Question 22 — Sparse nonnegative scalar

Let

and let (A=(Z)).

Compute (E[ZA]) and the MMSE.

8 Part III — (L^2) geometry and MMSE

8.1 Question 23 ★ — (L^2) as an inner-product space

Define

and its inner product and norm.

Why are random variables that are equal almost surely treated as the same element of (L^2)?

8.2 Question 24 — The observable subspace

For a sub-sigma-algebra (G), define

Prove it is a linear subspace of (L^2(,F,P)).

State the additional property needed before the orthogonal projection theorem applies.

8.3 Question 25 ★ — Projection theorem

State the orthogonal projection theorem for a closed linear subspace (M) of a Hilbert space (H).

Give both equivalent characterizations of the projection (P_Mx):

  1. closest point;
  2. orthogonality of the residual.

8.4 Question 26 ★ — Conditional expectation is the projection

Let (XL^2).

Prove that

satisfies

for every (VL^2(,G,P)).

First prove it for indicator functions, then simple functions, then general (V).

8.5 Question 27 ★ — Pythagorean risk decomposition

Let

and let (T) be any square-integrable (G)-measurable random vector.

Prove

Expand the square and prove that the cross term is exactly zero.

8.6 Question 28 ★ — MMSE decoder

Set

Using Questions 7 and 27, prove

State:

  1. the admissible class of (g);
  2. the integrability condition;
  3. the sense in which the minimizer is unique.

8.7 Question 29 ★ — Conditional covariance formula for (L(Q))

Prove

Your proof should be a chain of explicit equalities.

8.8 Question 30 ★ — Exact recoverability

Prove the equivalence

if and only if

if and only if there exists a measurable (g) such that

Explain why this is an almost-sure recoverability statement, not necessarily a statement that the deterministic map

is globally injective on all of (R^d).

8.9 Question 31 ★ — Arbitrary decoder versus linear decoder

Explain the difference between minimizing over:

and minimizing over:

Give a concrete example in which the MMSE decoder is nonlinear and therefore cannot be represented by an affine decoder.

8.10 Question 32 ★ — Gaussian rotational invariance

Assume

and (QO(d)).

Prove that (L(Q)) is independent of (Q).

Your proof must use:

  1. (Y=QZN(0,I_d));
  2. preservation of Euclidean norm by (Q);
  3. a bijection between decoders for (Z) and decoders for (Y).

9 Bridge problems toward the privileged-basis proof

These are not prerequisites. They test whether you can now begin the actual problem.

9.1 Bridge 1 — Identity basis for nonnegative latents

Assume

Prove

The sparsity and exponential assumptions are irrelevant for this step. Explain why.

9.2 Bridge 2 — Why one collision is insufficient

Someone argues:

I found (zz’) such that [ (Qz) = (Qz’). ] Therefore (L(Q)>0).

Give at least three reasons this argument is incomplete.

9.3 Bridge 3 — Correct positivity criterion

State a rigorous condition, in terms of conditional covariance, sufficient and necessary for

Then state a more concrete sufficient condition involving a positive-probability set of observations.

9.4 Bridge 4 — Forty-five-degree sparse case

Let (d=2) and

Let (Z_1,Z_2) be independent sparse nonnegative exponential variables.

Write

coordinatewise.

Identify a region of latent space on which one ReLU coordinate is zero and the other reveals only (Z_1+Z_2).

Formulate a rigorous route to proving that the conditional distribution of (Z) on this region has nonzero variance. You do not need to complete all measure-theoretic details, but you must identify exactly what must be proved.

10 Readiness criterion

There are (32) main questions, for (64) points.

You are ready to begin the privileged-basis proof when:

  • total score is at least (52/64);
  • no starred question receives (0);
  • Questions 27–30 can be reproduced without notes;
  • you can explain in one paragraph why the problem is fundamentally about the conditional spread of ReLU fibres under the law of (Z).

The answer key is a separate file. Do not open it until completing the closed-notes pass.
